text: Hi, welcome to This is my Architecture, coming to you from Zurich in Switzerland.
Today I have Olivier from Sonar Source with us.
Hi Olivier.
Hi Ross.
What can you tell us about Sonar Source?
Sonar Source was founded 13 years ago by three developers and the goal was to provide
code analysis solutions, so today we have three products, Sonar Lins, Sonar Cloud and Sonar
Cube and we support 27 programming languages.
And today we will speak about Sonar Cloud.
All right, great.
That sounds great.
Can you take us from the starting point of what we see on the board here?
Yes, exactly.
The starting point is developers.
As you know, developers write code and they push code into a GitHub repo most of the time.
So when a code change is pushed into this repo, it triggers an event and we capture this event
into this Lambda.
We do some triage, we do some analysis and then we create an analysis request.
It goes straight into this queue, an SQS queue.
Okay.
Why do you have SQS there?
Could you not speak directly to Fargate or even have Fargate picking up the message from
GitHub?
Now we have many reasons to use SQS.
The first reason is to level the load.
We don't want to overwhelm the Fargate cluster or the service.
And the second reason is for auto-scaling because this Fargate cluster is using auto-scaling
and it's plugged directly into this queue.
So the Fargate cluster, the service will pull the queue.
And then the first step is to clone the code.
I can imagine that can get pretty big.
You know, you've got no control over how big your customers' repos can be.
Exactly.
So the biggest code base is 9 megabytes.
And yes, it's pretty big.
And you use any external storage from Fargate in order to keep those repos?
No, no, no.
We stick to the fml storage.
For now it's good enough.
All right.
Great.
All right.
Let's see what happens next.
Once we have the code, we can analyze this code.
And once it's analyzed, we push an analysis report to this block.
And this CC2 instance is receiving reports through an API.
And then this report is stored into this database.
And it's in the database, we have this component implemented as a Fargate cluster.
And this component, we call it the Compute Engine, will pull the database to extract reports.
Okay.
So you're using this like a queuing mechanism?
Yes, exactly.
It's a temporary storage for the reports we get from this component.
And the Compute Engine here is pulling the database to extract the reports.
Once a report is extracted, we do a lot of computations, we compute a lot of code quality metrics.
And once it's done, we update the database and we create an analysis report here, ready to be consumed by users.
I would like to mention that we store also additional data here.
And this data is for optimization.
We do some search and we need to have shared data in an optimized format.
Okay.
And this is, is this an Amazon Elasticsearch?
No, no, not yet.
Today, it's an in-house Elasticsearch service, but we actually plan to move to the Amazon version.
Okay.
All right.
And then the next step from your user's perspective, I assume they want to be able to see the results of all of this hard work?
Yes.
Once it's done, they go to the UI and they call the UI or simply the API because you can extract the same data from the two different channels, the UI and the API.
Okay.
Great.
And what does this look like in the real world?
How, how, how much is this being used?
For Autoscan specifically, we analyze up to 150k analysis per week.
And SonarCloud is part of an ecosystem.
We basically support around 300,000 organizations.
Those are some, those are some really big numbers.
That's been, that's been great.
But what's, what's next?
What's, what's coming next for you?
Many things.
One of the, the first thing we want to do is to move to a managed and a service.
And a second important point would be to remove the queue we have in Aurora and to use a proper queue, an SQL queue.
Okay.
Okay.
Get some optimization there.
And any, anything from a product perspective?
From a product perspective for now, Autoscan does not support compile languages.
We support non compile languages, such as TypeScript, Python, and so on.
And the next step is to support Java and C-sharp.
Oh, Olivier, thank you for sharing that.
That was really interesting.
Welcome.
And thank you for watching.
This is my architecture.
The network that I've had today in Trivand store is actually showing up in the chat.
you
